---
title: "BASC vs the Standard Synthetic-Control Toolkit"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5.2
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 4.6
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    geometry: margin=1in
    colorlinks: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
import os, warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from IPython.display import Markdown

def _find_up(name, start, hops=6):
    d = os.path.abspath(start)
    for _ in range(hops):
        if os.path.exists(os.path.join(d, name)):
            return d
        d = os.path.dirname(d)
    return os.path.abspath(start)

ROOT = _find_up("basedata", os.getcwd())
BASE = os.path.join(ROOT, "basedata")
DATA = os.getcwd() if os.path.exists(os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "data", "basc_weights.csv")) \
    else os.path.join(ROOT, "review", "basc_westgermany")
DATA = os.path.join(DATA, "data")
```

## The question

Bayesian Adaptive Synthetic Control (BASC; *Bayesian Donor Set Selection in Synthetic Controls*) is presented as a competitive-or-superior synthetic-control estimator. Because the credibility of any synthetic control rests, to some degree, on how well it reproduces the treated unit's pre-intervention outcome trajectory, a minimum bar for such a claim is the pre-treatment fit for the new method compared to those of the standard tools in the SCM literature. This note compares BASC's in-sample (pre-1990) fit on West Germany against `mlsynth`'s own estimators on the identical data (the Abadie, Diamond & Hainmueller 2015 `repgermany` panel, original per-capita GDP scale), assembled with `mlsynth`'s `compare_estimators` utility. The `mlsynth` analyses run at render time; BASC is precomputed (its Gibbs sampler runs from the authors' R, `github.com/sll-lee/paper-BASC`).

## Specifications

Standard SCM (s-SCM, per the paper) is run two ways. Outcome-only matches the pre-1990 GDP path. The covariate specification is the canonical Abadie et al. (2015) predictor set:

- GDP per capita, inflation rate, trade openness, industry share — averaged
  **1981–1990**;
- investment rate and schooling — averaged **1980–1985**.

We estimate the fit via two bilevel solvers: `backend="malo"` (the Malo et al. 2024 optimum) and `backend="mscmt"` (MSCMT's nested differential-evolution search). The robust family is `CLUSTERSC` (RPCA-SC and PCR/RSC). As a benchmark outside the SCM family entirely, we also include `FDID`, the forward-selected difference-in-differences estimator of Li (2024).

```{python}
#| label: compare
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: false
from mlsynth import VanillaSC, CLUSTERSC, FDID, compare_estimators

# --- ADH 2015 West Germany covariate specification ---
COVS = ["gdp_avg", "infrate", "trade", "industry", "schooling", "invest80"]
WINDOWS = {"gdp_avg": (1981, 1990), "infrate": (1981, 1990), "trade": (1981, 1990),
           "industry": (1981, 1990), "schooling": (1980, 1985), "invest80": (1980, 1985)}
FIT_WINDOW = (1960, 1989)          # outcome-matching (pre-treatment) window

raw = pd.read_stata(os.path.join(BASE, "repgermany.dta"))
d = raw[["country", "year", "gdp", "infrate", "trade", "industry", "schooling", "invest80"]].copy()
d["gdp_avg"] = d["gdp"]             # GDP per capita as an averaged predictor
d["treat"] = ((d.country == "West Germany") & (d.year >= 1990)).astype(int)

base = dict(df=d, outcome="gdp", treat="treat", unitid="country", time="year",
            display_graphs=False)
cov = dict(covariates=COVS, covariate_windows=WINDOWS, fit_window=FIT_WINDOW, seed=0)

# One panel, several specifications -> compare_estimators does the rest
cmp = compare_estimators({
    "VanillaSC (outcome-only)":    VanillaSC({**base}),
    "VanillaSC (malo, ADH cov)":   VanillaSC({**base, "backend": "malo",  **cov}),
    "VanillaSC (mscmt, ADH cov)":  VanillaSC({**base, "backend": "mscmt", **cov}),
    "CLUSTERSC (RPCA-SC)":         CLUSTERSC({**base, "method": "rpca", "rpca_method": "PCP"}),
    "CLUSTERSC (PCR/RSC)":         CLUSTERSC({**base, "method": "PCR"}),
    "FDID (Forward DID)":          FDID({**base}),
}, show_bands=False)

# uniform-weight diagnostic (what a naive equal-weighted "SC" would fit)
wide = d.pivot(index="year", columns="country", values="gdp"); pre = wide.index < 1990
order = [c for c in wide.columns if c != "West Germany"]; X = wide[order].to_numpy()
obs_pre = wide["West Germany"].to_numpy()[pre]
uniform_rmse = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((obs_pre - X[pre] @ np.full(len(order), 1/len(order)))**2)))
```


```{python}
#| label: load-basc
basc_cf = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_counterfactual.csv"))        # 2000/2000
basc_cf500 = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_counterfactual_500.csv")) # 500/500
basc_w = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_weights.csv"))
```

## In-sample (pre-1990) RMSE by method

```{python}
#| label: tbl-rmse
tbl = (cmp.summary.rename(columns={"pre_rmse": "In-sample RMSE", "att": "ATT"})
       .reset_index().rename(columns={"method": "Method"}))
tbl = tbl[["Method", "In-sample RMSE", "ATT"]]

# BASC in-sample RMSE and post-1990 ATT from each saved posterior counterfactual
# (ATT = mean(observed - synthetic) over 1990+, the same convention as cmp.summary).
def _basc_rmse_att(cf):
    pre, post = cf["year"] < 1990, cf["year"] >= 1990
    rmse = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((cf.loc[pre, "observed"] - cf.loc[pre, "synthetic"]) ** 2)))
    att = float((cf.loc[post, "observed"] - cf.loc[post, "synthetic"]).mean())
    return rmse, att

rmse_2000, att_2000 = _basc_rmse_att(basc_cf)
rmse_500,  att_500  = _basc_rmse_att(basc_cf500)
# 25000/25000: reported from a single long offline chain (seed 200). Its posterior
# counterfactual is not stored or plotted; the exact script and the commit that
# produced these two numbers are in the reproducibility appendix at the end.
rmse_25k, att_25k = 195.516, -389.799
basc_rows = pd.DataFrame([
    {"Method": "BASC (25000 / 25000 MCMC)", "In-sample RMSE": rmse_25k,  "ATT": att_25k},
    {"Method": "BASC (2000 / 2000 MCMC)",   "In-sample RMSE": rmse_2000, "ATT": att_2000},
    {"Method": "BASC (500 / 500 MCMC)",     "In-sample RMSE": rmse_500,  "ATT": att_500},
])
full = pd.concat([tbl, basc_rows], ignore_index=True).sort_values("In-sample RMSE").reset_index(drop=True)
Markdown(full.round(1).to_markdown(index=False))
```

Quite generally, lower in-sample RMSE is better. Every comparison estimator — including the *robust* `CLUSTERSC` methods, which deliberately denoise rather than chase in-sample fit, attains a materially tighter pre-treatment fit than BASC. BASC's pre-1990 RMSE sits in a ~170–195 band across the three chain lengths (500/500, 2000/2000, 25000/25000) — roughly 3x the plain `VanillaSC` fit (60.8), and about twice the `CLUSTERSC` (88.6 / 98.0) and `FDID` (83.4) fits. Lengthening the MCMC does not close the gap; if anything the best-mixed 25000/25000 chain fits worst, so the shortfall is not a burn-in or short-chain artifact. For reference, *uniform* (1/16) donor weights (essentially what the authors call s-SCM in their table 7 column number 4) fit ~21x worse (RMSE ~1289).

## Observed vs synthetic West Germany

```{python}
#| label: fig-paths
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
cmp.plot(ax=ax)                                   # mlsynth methods + observed
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["synthetic"], color="#d62728", lw=2.2,
        label="BASC", zorder=5)
ax.axvspan(basc_cf["year"].min(), 1990, color="0.92", zorder=0)
ax.axvline(1990, color="grey", ls=":", lw=1)
ax.set_title("West Germany: observed vs synthetic counterfactuals")
ax.set_xlabel("year"); ax.set_ylabel("GDP per capita")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left"); ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

The gap of interest is inside the shaded pre-1990 window: the standard estimators track observed West Germany closely, while BASC departs from it *before* treatment (at least, using the RMSE as a guide).

## BASC counterfactual with its 95% credible band

```{python}
#| label: fig-basc-ci
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.axvspan(basc_cf["year"].min(), 1990, color="0.92", zorder=0)
ax.fill_between(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["lo95"], basc_cf["hi95"], color="#d62728", alpha=0.15, label="BASC 95% CI")
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["synthetic"], color="#d62728", lw=2, label="BASC synthetic")
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["observed"], color="black", lw=2.4, label="West Germany (observed)")
ax.axvline(1990, color="grey", ls=":", lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("year"); ax.set_ylabel("GDP per capita")
ax.set_title("BASC posterior counterfactual (2000/2000)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left"); ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

My other main concern with this manuscript is that uniform solution for standard SCM. The authors, in Table 7 column 4, claim that this is the standard SCM solution. But this cannot be true: above, we reviewed the weights and the RMSE for the standard ADH method. The weights obtained from the ADH specification are sparse, even if they disagree on exactly how much weight a donor gets. But in this case, the solution is said to be uniform, which cannot be true. The sticking point for all of this is that the authors write, specifically "we have removed the normalization step and rerun the entire west germany analysis using the original per capita GDP scale, consistent with the standard setup of ADH 2010/2015... all empirical results have been updated. The revised results lead to the same substantive conclusions as those reported in the previous version."


While the reported results from BASC appear to be roughly consistent with my replication, my concern is that the result of BASC is consistently imprecise compared to its comparison methods, making the advantages of BASC over RSC/RPCA-SC unclear (in the empirical comparisons).

## A second canonical case: the Basque Country

To check that the West Germany result is not a quirk of one dataset, I repeat the exercise on the other canonical synthetic-control application: Abadie and Gardeazabal's (2003) study of ETA terrorism in the Basque Country (`basedata/basque_data.csv` — 17 Spanish regions, real GDP per capita, 1955–1997, treatment dated 1975). The comparison is outcome-only: each method matches the pre-1975 Basque GDP path using the other regions as donors, exactly as BASC does, so the in-sample RMSE is a clean, like-for-like measure of pre-treatment fit.

```{python}
#| label: compare-basque
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: false
basq = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(BASE, "basque_data.csv"))
bq = dict(df=basq, outcome="gdpcap", treat="terrorism", unitid="regionname",
          time="year", display_graphs=False)                  # treat=1 for Basque from 1975
cmp_bq = compare_estimators({
    "VanillaSC (outcome-only)": VanillaSC({**bq}),
    "CLUSTERSC (RPCA-SC)":      CLUSTERSC({**bq, "method": "rpca", "rpca_method": "PCP"}),
    "CLUSTERSC (PCR/RSC)":      CLUSTERSC({**bq, "method": "PCR"}),
    "FDID (Forward DID)":       FDID({**bq}),
}, show_bands=False)
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-basque
tb = (cmp_bq.summary.rename(columns={"pre_rmse": "In-sample RMSE", "att": "ATT"})
      .reset_index().rename(columns={"method": "Method"}))[["Method", "In-sample RMSE", "ATT"]]
# BASC on Basque: offline chains (seed 200, treatment 1975) -- see the reproducibility appendix
basc_bq = pd.DataFrame([
    {"Method": "BASC (20000 / 20000 MCMC)", "In-sample RMSE": 0.6099, "ATT": -3.1382},
    {"Method": "BASC (5000 / 5000 MCMC)",   "In-sample RMSE": 0.6221, "ATT": -3.2118},
])
full_bq = pd.concat([tb, basc_bq], ignore_index=True).sort_values("In-sample RMSE").reset_index(drop=True)
Markdown(full_bq.round(3).to_markdown(index=False))
```

The pattern is the same, only sharper. Every standard estimator — including `FDID`, which is not a synthetic control — fits the pre-1975 path several times more tightly than BASC: its in-sample RMSE (~0.61) is roughly 7x `VanillaSC` (0.084) and 8x `FDID` (0.076), and lengthening the chain from 5000 to 20000 barely moves it (0.622 to 0.610). The consequence for the effect estimate is stark. The standard toolkit agrees on an ATT of about -0.7 to -0.9 — the ~10% GDP loss Abadie and Gardeazabal report and that the literature cites — whereas BASC returns roughly -3.2, three to four times larger. Because BASC cannot track the pre-1975 series, its synthetic Basque sits well above the observed path, and most of that estimated "effect" is the pre-treatment miss carried forward, not the intervention.

## Bottom line

To be clear: I recognize that in-sample fit is not the sole determining factor in the validity of a novel SCM. For example, in the original Robust SC and Robust PCA SC papers by Amjad and Bayani respectively, the authors remark that their denoising solutions are desirable because they attain similar results as the original method without the need to match on predictors. As we can see here, these results hold even though PCR, clustered PCR/RPCA SC attain slightly worse in-sample fit compared to the baseline model from ADH 2015.

However, it is not my belief that BASC attains comparable in-sample fit compared to the baseline toolkit. This holds even on the original, un-normalized per-capita GDP scale the authors adopt in the revision — the very data on which they report the same substantive conclusions. On that data, BASC has an in-sample RMSE that is ~2–3x that of `VanillaSC` and worse than the robust `CLUSTERSC` estimators — indeed worse than a forward-selected difference-in-differences (`FDID`), which is not even a synthetic control — on top of the fact that it excludes the canonical Austria. The paper claims competitiveness to the baseline toolkit in simulation studies, but the competitiveness on the canonical datasets has not yet been demonstrated.

Taken together, the picture is consistent: BASC does not compete with the standard toolkit on in-sample fit — it does not even match a forward-selected difference-in-differences — and that lack of fit appears to feed through to the estimated effect, whose magnitude (roughly -390 to -700 across chain lengths) is neither stable nor close to the -1300 to -2000 that the standard estimators agree on. The same pattern recurs on the Basque Country, and more sharply: BASC fits ~7x looser than the whole toolkit and returns an ATT (~-3.2) three to four times the ~10% loss the standard estimators and the original Abadie–Gardeazabal study agree on — so this is not an artifact of one dataset. BASC is also markedly more expensive to compute (an MCMC sampler against one-shot convex solves). What remains is a method that performs well in theory and in the paper's Monte Carlo, but whose empirical usefulness — at least as presented here — has not been demonstrated.

## Reproducibility: the offline BASC chains

Most BASC rows are regenerated at render time from saved posterior counterfactuals
(West Germany 500/500 and 2000/2000). The remaining rows come from single offline
chains whose posterior paths are not stored or plotted — only their summary numbers
are reported: West Germany 25000/25000 (in-sample RMSE 195.516, post-1990 ATT
-389.799), and the Basque Country 5000/5000 and 20000/20000 (RMSE 0.622 / 0.610,
ATT -3.212 / -3.138). The exact script and the data each reads are given below so
they can be reproduced against a known state of this repository.

```{python}
#| label: stamp
#| echo: false
import subprocess
def _git(*a):
    try:
        return subprocess.run(["git", *a], capture_output=True, text=True, cwd=ROOT).stdout.strip() or "unknown"
    except Exception:
        return "unknown"
Markdown(f"Reproduced against `mlsynth` at commit `{_git('rev-parse', '--short', 'HEAD')}` "
         f"({_git('log', '-1', '--format=%ci')}).")
```

Every run uses the authors' own sampler (`safe_chol` ... `run_basc_chain` from
`github.com/sll-lee/paper-BASC`, sourced as `basc_funcs.R`), and is identical to
`scripts/basc_run.R` except for the inputs, the treatment index, and the chain
length. The West Germany 25000/25000 run is shown below (not executed); the Basque
runs use the same script with the substitutions noted after it.

```r
# BASC on West Germany, 25000 burn-in + 25000 sampling (single chain, seed 200).
#   -> in-sample (pre-1990) RMSE = 195.516,  post-1990 ATT = -389.799
suppressMessages(library(MASS))
rinvgamma <- function(n, shape, scale) 1 / rgamma(n, shape = shape, rate = scale)
rtmvnorm <- function(n, mean, sigma, upper, ...) {   # q=1, diagonal, upper-truncated
  sdv <- sqrt(diag(as.matrix(sigma))); qd <- length(mean); out <- numeric(qd)
  for (i in seq_len(qd)) {
    p <- pnorm((upper[i] - mean[i]) / sdv[i])
    out[i] <- mean[i] + sdv[i] * qnorm(runif(1, 0, max(p, 1e-12)))
  }
  matrix(out, nrow = 1)
}
rdist <- function(x1, x2 = NULL) {
  x1 <- as.matrix(x1); if (is.null(x2)) x2 <- x1 else x2 <- as.matrix(x2)
  outer(seq_len(nrow(x1)), seq_len(nrow(x2)),
        Vectorize(function(i, j) sqrt(sum((x1[i, ] - x2[j, ])^2))))
}
source("basc_funcs.R")                        # authors' safe_chol .. run_basc_chain

y  <- as.numeric(read.csv("y.csv", header = FALSE)[, 1])    # West Germany GDP (44)
x  <- as.matrix(read.csv("x.csv", header = FALSE))          # 16 donors x 44 years
vt <- seq_len(length(y)); Dt <- as.integer(vt >= 31)        # treatment from 1990
j <- ncol(x); t <- length(vt); N <- 25000; nburn <- 25000   # (paper uses 500000/500000)

chain <- run_basc_chain(seed = 200, y = y, x = x, vt = vt, Dt = Dt, N = N, nburn = nburn, q = 1)

g <- chain$gamma.sample; u <- chain$u.sample
beta <- matrix(0, N, j); for (i in 1:N) beta[i, ] <- (g[i, ] * u[i, ]) / sum(g[i, ] * u[i, ])
y.samp <- matrix(0, N, t)
for (i in 1:N) y.samp[i, ] <- as.numeric(x %*% beta[i, ] + chain$f.sample[i, ])
cf <- colMeans(y.samp); pre <- vt < 31; post <- vt >= 31
cat(sprintf("in-sample RMSE = %.3f   post-1990 ATT = %.3f\n",
            sqrt(mean((y[pre] - cf[pre])^2)), mean(y[post] - cf[post])))
```

The Basque rows use the same script with `y` and `x` exported from
`basedata/basque_data.csv` (the Basque Country as the treated unit, the 16 other
regions as donors), the treatment index changed to `Dt <- as.integer(vt >= 21)`
(year 1975), and `N = nburn` set to 5000 and 20000.
